figures.name: Cao Đài (The Supreme Being)
figures.gloss: The Supreme Being, God the Father, whose title translates to 'Highest Tower' or 'Highest Palace'. He reveals himself as the singular, ultimate source of all major world religions, returning to unite them into one final, universal faith.
figures.facet: He operates not as a distant prime mover, but as an active, micromanaging executive of the cosmos, directly dictating architectural blueprints, clerical promotions, and geopolitical commentary through séance.
figures.provenance: 'Thánh Ngôn Hiệp Tuyển' (Collection of Divine Messages, 1928); original 1926 séance revelations.

figures.name: Thiên Nhãn (The Divine Eye)
figures.gloss: The central object of worship, specifically the Left Eye. It represents the Yang principle, the sun, and the universal, penetrating gaze of the Creator observing the individual conscience.
figures.facet: Bypasses human anthropomorphism for a surreal, disembodied organ of surveillance and enlightenment, serving as the physical focal point that draws worshippers into the grand syncretic cathedral.
figures.provenance: Ngô Văn Chiêu's vision on Phú Quốc island (1921).

figures.name: Diêu Trì Kim Mẫu (Holy Mother of the Jade Pool)
figures.gloss: The supreme feminine principle (Yin), responsible for birthing the spiritual bodies of all living beings and guiding their emotional and spiritual salvation.
figures.facet: While the Father rules the strict, bureaucratic hierarchy of the church, the Mother exists entirely outside this administrative chain of command, presiding over a separate, deeply emotional cult of maternal grace.
figures.provenance: Adapted from the Taoist Xi Wangmu; 'Kinh Thiên Đạo Và Thế Đạo' (Prayers of the Heavenly and Earthly Way).

figures.name: Tam Thánh (The Three Saints)
figures.gloss: Victor Hugo, Sun Yat-sen, and Trạng Trình Nguyễn Bỉnh Khiêm. This triad—a French romantic poet, a Chinese republican revolutionary, and a Vietnamese prophet—serve as spiritual vanguards who signed the divine pact between God and Humanity.
figures.facet: Collapses the sacred and secular completely. Anti-colonial literature and democratic revolution are literally canonized as divine mechanics, elevating recent political actors to cosmic mediators.
figures.provenance: The famous mural at the entrance of the Tây Ninh Holy See; 1920s séance transcripts.

figures.name: Cơ Bút (The Beaked Basket)
figures.gloss: The 'corbeille-à-bec', a specialized planchette held by two mediums to channel spirit-writing. It transcribes poems, decrees, and laws from the spiritual realm in French, Vietnamese, and Chinese.
figures.facet: Strips away the ecstatic, frenzied trance of traditional shamanism, replacing it with a sober, highly literate bureaucratic process. Revelation becomes a formalized dictation of institutional law.
figures.provenance: Early Caodaist spiritist sessions (1925-1926); 'Pháp Chánh Truyền' (The Religious Constitution).

figures.name: Tam Kỳ Phổ Độ (The Third Universal Amnesty)
figures.gloss: The theological epoch declaring that previous religions (the First and Second Amnesties) failed due to geographical isolation and human corruption, necessitating one final, unifying revelation.
figures.facet: Replaces apocalyptic judgment with an 'amnesty'. The end of the cycle is not a destructive purge, but a massive, urgent diplomatic effort by Heaven to pardon and assimilate all divided faiths.
figures.provenance: Foundational revelations to Phạ́m Công Tắc and Cao Quỳnh Cư (1926).

figures.name: Bát Quái Đài (The Eight-Trigram Palace)
figures.gloss: The invisible legislative branch of the universe, inhabited by God, Buddhas, Immortals, and Saints, who formulate the divine laws executed by the earthly church.
figures.facet: Heaven is explicitly structured as a modern constitutional government. The spiritual realm acts as a legislative senate, perfectly mirroring and commanding the earthly administrative branches below it.
figures.provenance: 'Pháp Chánh Truyền' (The Religious Constitution of Caodaism).

figures.name: Lý Thái Bạch (Li Bai)
figures.gloss: The 8th-century Chinese Tang dynasty poet, chosen by Cao Đài to serve as the 'Giáo Tông' (Spiritual Pope) of the Great Faith and primary liaison to the earthly realm.
figures.facet: God bypassed traditional ascetics and martial heroes to select an infamously drunken, rebellious romantic poet as the supreme, unyielding enforcer of universal administrative law.
figures.provenance: 'Thánh Ngôn Hiệp Tuyển'; séance records installing him as the invisible Pope.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The supreme Father and Mother, ruling the Yang and Yin principles, respectively handling institutional law and emotional salvation.
Intra-tradition: The Supreme Being and his designated, physical focal point for earthly worship.
Intra-tradition: The heavenly legislative palace and its Spiritual Pope who transmits its laws to the physical church.
Intra-tradition: The Saints who deliver the divine messages and the exact mechanical instrument used to transcribe them.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cao Đài doctrine explicitly identifies its Supreme Being as the Jade Emperor/Shangdi of the Chinese pantheon.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cao Đài doctrine explicitly names Jehovah as an earlier, localized manifestation of the Supreme Being.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Allah is explicitly claimed in Caodaist texts as one of the Father's prior divine identities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Holy Mother is formally defined as the absolute manifestation of the Yin principle in the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Mother's cult is heavily syncretized with Quan Am (Avalokitesvara), sharing her role of ultimate maternal compassion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Jesus Christ is explicitly incorporated into Caodaist history as the central redeeming figure of the Second Universal Amnesty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Caodaist theological timeline fundamentally operates on adapted Buddhist kalpas (cosmic cycles) to justify its Amnesties.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The omnipresent, disembodied solar eye that observes all creation, serving as the ultimate icon of the supreme deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A final, cosmic era of renovation designed not to destroy the world, but to restore and unite a fractured humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme scribe and literary intellect who manages the administrative and legal liaison between heaven and earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Prophetic figures who consciously and explicitly attempt to stitch all prior, geographically distinct religions into a single tapestry.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Highly formalized, technical mechanisms of divination used to establish state law and construct religious architecture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An objective, mechanical system for retrieving divine commands that removes the subjectivity of human ecstatic trance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The structured, hierarchical fullness of the divine realm, inhabited by eons/immortals radiating from the central Godhead.

proposedArchetypes.name: Bureaucratic Heaven
proposedArchetypes.gloss: The conceptualization of the divine realm not as a state of ecstatic bliss, but as a vast, structured civil service or parliament with ministries, titles, and legislation.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Necessary to capture the distinct flavor of the Caodaist Bát Quái Đài and the broader Chinese celestial bureaucracy it draws from, where salvation is an administrative process.

proposedArchetypes.name: Spirit-Writing Mechanism
proposedArchetypes.gloss: An objective, physical tool (planchette, basket, or pendulum) used to transcribe exact, verbatim text from the divine, minimizing human ecstatic interpretation.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Crucial for traditions where revelation is primarily literary and dictated through objects, linking the Caodai corbeille-à-bec to Chinese fuji and 19th-century Spiritism.
